summary: Fernando Pérez argues that open source software in science is far more than code — it encompasses services, standards, protocols, and most critically, human communities. He traces the history and impact of Jupyter and the scientific Python stack through landmark achievements like LIGO and the Event Horizon Telescope, while highlighting the severe sustainability crisis facing these tools. Despite powering trillions of dollars in industry value and underpinning the most important scientific results of the decade, these projects remain chronically underfunded and their creators face hostile academic incentive structures. Pérez calls for just 0.1% of the federal R&D budget to be directed toward core open source scientific infrastructure, while warning that volunteer-only models exclude underrepresented groups.
key_points: Open source projects are complex human organizations requiring governance, community management, and standards — not just code on GitHub
The scientific Python stack (NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib, Jupyter) underpins landmark results like LIGO's gravitational wave detection and the Event Horizon Telescope black hole image
Academic incentives around 'novel and transformative' work are poisonous to sustaining the domain-agnostic software backbone that all science depends on
Creators of foundational tools like NumPy, Matplotlib, and Sage have been forced out of academia or had to abandon open source work due to lack of career support
Jupyter's success came from distilling ideas into open protocols and standards, enabling 100+ language backends through careful generalization with just two or three community partners
money_quotes: The managing and the fostering and the creation and the healthy nurturing of a community is probably the hardest and yet the most critical part of this whole story.

Those two stupid words are the most poisonous piece of information that has ever been injected into the federal funding system because they justify this kind of stuff happening over and over, and literally billions of dollars have been sacrificed on the altar of those two stupid words.
The notion that volunteers are going to do this job is a great way to make sure that only people who can afford to volunteer do the job, which means typically less women and minorities.
I'm the statistics professor who didn't know what a t-test was when I was hired, and that's not a joke. It's a statement of fact.
We've managed to engineer a system of incentives that makes exactly the right things the hardest things to do, and that's a problem.

If we want a voice in the development of these things, the scientific community and the funders of the scientific community have to actually invest, because you get a voice at the table by participating, not simply by saying that you should have a voice.
All of these impacts don't just happen because you're very smart and have a GitHub account. That's not how it works.
As a climate scientist, you shouldn't have to become an Amazon engineer. You should just zoom on your data and something should do that job for you.
key_insight: The domain-agnostic software backbone that all modern science depends on is nobody's institutional responsibility, creating a sustainability crisis that can only be solved by recognizing open source projects as real human organizations deserving of real investment.
